definitions.definition: A social process by which individuals and groups obtain what they need through creating, offering, and freely exchanging products and services of value with others.
The set of controllable variables a firm blends to produce the desired response: Product, Price, Place, Promotion.
Anything offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a need or want.
Giving a distinct name, symbol, or design to identify and differentiate a product from competitors. Components: Brand Name, Brand Mark, Trademark.
The spoken part of a brand — the word or phrase. Examples: Nike, Amul, Apple.
The visual symbol or design of a brand that cannot be spoken. Example: Nike's swoosh, Apple's apple logo.
Legal protection of a brand under law, indicated by ™ or ®. Prevents others from using the same brand identity.
All written information on the product or package — brand name, ingredients, MRP, manufacturing/expiry date, nutritional info, statutory warnings.
Primary (immediate container — toothpaste tube), Secondary (additional protection — cardboard box), Transportation (for shipping — cartons).
Paid, non-personal communication through mass media (TV, newspaper, online) to reach a large audience. Wide reach but impersonal.
Face-to-face interaction between seller and buyer. Most effective for high-value, complex products. Immediate feedback.
Short-term incentives to boost sales — free samples, discounts, contests, lucky draws, cashbacks, buy-one-get-one.
Building goodwill and positive image through press conferences, sponsorships, special events, community programmes. Not paid advertising — earned media.

scenarios.scenario: A company offers free samples at shopping malls and runs a 'Buy 2 Get 1 Free' offer.
scenarios.principle: Short-term incentives to boost immediate sales

scenarios.scenario: A pharma company sends medical representatives to visit doctors and explain new drugs.
scenarios.principle: Face-to-face interaction for complex, high-value products

scenarios.scenario: Coca-Cola runs a 30-second TV commercial during the IPL cricket match.
scenarios.principle: Paid, non-personal communication through mass media

scenarios.scenario: A company sponsors a marathon and holds a press conference about its CSR initiatives.
scenarios.principle: Building goodwill through earned media, not paid ads

scenarios.scenario: A health drink is priced low to capture maximum market share quickly.
scenarios.principle: Low price strategy to gain market share

scenarios.scenario: Products move from Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer for consumer goods.
Dell sells laptops directly to consumers through its website — no middlemen.
A toothpaste has the tube (primary), cardboard box (secondary), and shipping carton (transportation).
A company researches customer needs first, then designs a product to satisfy those needs.
scenarios.principle: Starts before production — customer needs drive product design

scenarios.scenario: A salesperson pushes existing inventory to customers regardless of what they actually need.
scenarios.principle: Focus on existing products, not customer needs
